# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT742]**
**Define the term "food additive" as per FSSAI Regulations, 2011. List any four functional classes of food additives with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** As per Regulation 3.1.1 of the FSSAI (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011, a food additive is any substance not normally consumed as a food itself, and not normally used as a typical ingredient, whose intentional addition to food for a technological purpose results (or may reasonably be expected to result) in it or its by-products becoming a component of or affecting the characteristics of that food.
- **Four functional classes with examples (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
  1. **Colours** — Tartrazine (INS 102)
  2. **Preservatives** — Sodium Benzoate (INS 211)
  3. **Antioxidants** — BHA (INS 320)
  4. **Emulsifiers** — Lecithin (INS 322)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEFTT742]**
**What is the International Numbering System (INS) for food additives? Write the INS numbers for any two synthetic colours and two preservatives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **INS explained (1 mark):** The INS is a numerical system for identifying food additives, developed by the Codex Alimentarius Commission and adopted in India by FSSAI. It is used on food labels worldwide to ensure unambiguous identification of additives.
- **Synthetic Colours (1 mark):** Tartrazine — **INS 102**; Sunset Yellow FCF — **INS 110**.
- **Preservatives (1 mark):** Benzoic Acid — **INS 210**; Sodium Benzoate — **INS 211**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT742, Module 2]**

#### (a) Classify the different types of food additives permitted under FSSAI Regulations, 2011. Discuss in detail the permitted **colours** and **preservatives** along with their maximum permitted levels in specified food categories. (7 marks)

#### (b) Explain the concept of **antioxidants** and **emulsifiers** as food additives. Describe their classification, mechanism of action, and any two examples from each class with INS numbers. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. Classification of food additives (2 marks):**
FSSAI Regulations 2011 classify additives based on **technological function**:
- Colours, Preservatives, Antioxidants, Emulsifiers, Stabilisers, Acidulants, Flavour Enhancers, Sweeteners, Anticaking Agents, Raising Agents, and several others.

**2. Colours (2.5 marks):**
- **Synthetic colours** (Schedule V of FSSAI 2011): Tartrazine (INS 102), Sunset Yellow FCF (INS 110), Carmoisine (INS 122), Ponceau 4R (INS 124), Erythrosine (INS 127), Indigo Carmine (INS 132), Brilliant Blue FCF (INS 133), Fast Green FCF (INS 143).
- **Natural colours**: Curcumin (INS 100(i)), Caramel (INS 150), Carmine (INS 120), Annatto (INS 160b), Beta Carotene (INS 160a).
- **Lake colours**: water-insoluble dyes on alumina substrate.
- **Permitted levels**: For synthetic colours in carbonated water — **100 mg/kg**; in sweets, ice cream, confectionery — typically **200 mg/kg**.
- **Prohibited colours** (e.g., Rhodamine B, Auramine, Malachite Green) — their use is a punishable offence.

**3. Preservatives (2.5 marks):**
- **Class I preservatives** (common salt, sugar, vinegar, spices) — permitted in any quantity (GMP-based).
- **Class II preservatives**:
  - **Benzoates (INS 210, 211)** — for fruit juices, squashes: **600 mg/kg**; for carbonated beverages: **150 mg/kg**.
  - **Sorbates (INS 200, 202)** — for cheese, bakery: **1000 mg/kg**.
  - **Sulphites (INS 220–224)** — for dried fruits: **2000 mg/kg**; for fruit juice (reconstituted): **100 mg/kg**; for wine: **350 mg/kg**.
  - **Nitrites (INS 249, 250)** — for cured meats: **200 mg/kg**.

##### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**1. Antioxidants — definition & classification (3 marks):**
- Antioxidants are substances that **retard oxidative deterioration** of food, especially lipid-rich foods.
- **Natural antioxidants:** Ascorbic Acid (INS 300), Tocopherols (INS 307), Lecithin (INS 322), Rosemary Extract (INS 392).
- **Synthetic antioxidants:** BHA (INS 320), BHT (INS 321), TBHQ (INS 319), Propyl Gallate (INS 310).
- **Mechanism:** They donate hydrogen atoms to free radicals, terminating the lipid peroxidation chain reaction. Total antioxidant limit in edible oils is **200 mg/kg** (with TBHQ alone capped at **120 mg/kg**).

**2. Emulsifiers — definition & classification (3 marks):**
- Emulsifiers help form and stabilise mixtures of immiscible phases (oil and water) by reducing interfacial tension.
- **Natural emulsifiers:** Lecithin (INS 322) from soy or egg yolk.
- **Synthetic emulsifiers:** Mono-/Di-glycerides (INS 471), Polysorbates (INS 432–436), Sodium Stearoyl Lactylate — SSL (INS 481), DATEM (INS 472e).
- **Mechanism:** Orient at the oil–water interface with the hydrophilic head in water and lipophilic tail in oil. HLB value determines suitability: O/W emulsions (HLB 8–18), W/O emulsions (HLB 0–6).

**3. Examples with INS numbers (1 mark):**
- Lecithin — INS 322; Mono-/Di-glycerides — INS 471; Polysorbate 80 — INS 433; SSL — INS 481; DATEM — INS 472e.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PEFTT742, Module 2]**

#### (a) Explain the **International Numbering System (INS)** for food additives. Discuss the FSSAI regulatory framework for permitting additives in India, with a note on the role of the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** and **JECFA**. (7 marks)

#### (b) Write detailed notes on the use of **emulsifiers and stabilisers** in the food industry. With suitable examples, explain the **HLB concept** and its application in the selection of emulsifiers. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**1. International Numbering System (2 marks):**
- The INS is a numerical identification system for food additives, developed by the **Codex Committee on Food Additives (CCFA)** under the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**.
- Each additive is assigned a unique number (e.g., Sodium Benzoate — 211, BHA — 320). It is used globally on ingredient labels to identify additives unambiguously.

**2. Role of CAC and JECFA (2 marks):**
- **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC):** Established by FAO and WHO in 1963. Develops harmonised international food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice, including the **General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA)**.
- **JECFA (Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives):** An international scientific expert committee that evaluates the safety of food additives. It establishes the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** for each additive, expressed in mg/kg body weight/day.

**3. FSSAI regulatory framework (3 marks):**
- India follows the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** and the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**.
- The **Scientific Panel on Food Additives** of FSSAI reviews safety data, conducts risk assessments, and recommends additives for inclusion in the Schedules.
- Additives are listed in **Schedule IV through Schedule IX** of the 2011 Regulations, specifying the **name, INS number, food category, and maximum permitted level**.
- All permitted additives must be declared on the label with their **class name and INS number** (e.g., *"Contains Class II Preservatives (INS 211)"*).
- Use of **non-permitted additives** is punishable under **Section 26** of the FSS Act, 2006.

##### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**1. Emulsifiers in food industry (2.5 marks):**
- Used in **bakery** (SSL, DATEM for dough strengthening), **dairy** (Lecithin, Mono-/Di-glycerides in ice cream), **confectionery** (Lecithin in chocolate), **sauces and dressings** (Lecithin, Polysorbates).
- Functions: Reduce interfacial tension, improve texture, prevent phase separation, and enhance shelf life.

**2. Stabilisers in food industry (2 marks):**
- Used in **ice cream** (Carrageenan, Sodium CMC, Xanthan Gum) to prevent ice-crystal growth; in **jams and jellies** (Pectin, Agar) for gel formation; in **sauces** (Xanthan Gum, Guar Gum) for viscosity control; in **processed meats** (Carrageenan) for water-holding capacity.

**3. HLB Concept and application (2.5 marks):**
- HLB (Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance) is a numerical scale (0–20) that quantifies the relative affinity of an emulsifier for water vs. oil.
- **HLB 0–6:** W/O emulsions (e.g., Sorbitan tristearate, HLB = 2).
- **HLB 7–9:** Wetting agents.
- **HLB 8–18:** O/W emulsions (e.g., Polysorbate 80, HLB = 15).
- **HLB 13–15:** Detergents, solubilising agents.
- **Application:** A food technologist first determines the required HLB for the food system (by trial blends) and then selects emulsifiers with HLB close to the required value. In ice cream (O/W), emulsifiers like **Mono-/Di-glycerides (HLB ≈ 3–4)** and **Polysorbate 80 (HLB ≈ 15)** are blended to achieve a **composite HLB** of 8–12 suitable for the system.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make in PEFTT742 (Module 2) — and How to Lose Fewer Marks**
>
> 1. **Wrong citation of INS numbers** — Students often confuse *Sunset Yellow FCF (INS 110)* with *Tartrazine (INS 102)* or *Allura Red (INS 129)*. Always verify the **INS number** from the FSSAI schedule before writing. (**Loss: 1–2 marks per wrong INS**)
> 2. **Confusing emulsifiers with stabilisers** — Emulsifiers stabilise *emulsions* (oil + water); stabilisers/thickeners increase *viscosity* or form *gels*. They are **separate functional classes** under FSSAI. (**Loss: 1 mark**)
> 3. **Not stating numerical limits** — A 7-mark answer on preservatives that does not mention the **mg/kg permitted limit** (e.g., SO₂ = 100 mg/kg in juice) is considered incomplete. (**Loss: 2–3 marks**)
> 4. **Conflating antioxidants with preservatives** — Antioxidants retard *oxidation*; preservatives retard *microbial growth*. Do not interchange. (**Loss: 1 mark**)
> 5. **Omitting the label-declaration requirement** — Forgetting to mention that the additive must be **declared on the label with its class and INS number** results in a 1-mark loss on labelling-related questions.
> 6. **Using outdated terms** — The FSSAI 2011 Regulations replaced the older **PFA (Prevention of Food Adulteration) Act, 1954**. Mentioning PFA limits without referencing FSSAI 2011 is partial credit only.
> 7. **Skipping the regulatory reference** — Each answer should cite the **specific Regulation number** (e.g., "as per Regulation 3.3 of the FSSAI 2011 Regulations") for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for PEFTT742 Module 2**

- ✅ **Definition:** Food additive as per **Regulation 3.1.1 of FSSAI 2011** — substance intentionally added for technological purpose, not consumed as food itself.
- ✅ **INS** = International Numbering System (Codex). Used for global additive identification.
- ✅ **Colours:** Synthetic (Schedule V) and Natural — e.g., Tartrazine **102**, Sunset Yellow **110**, Curcumin **100(i)**, Caramel **150**. Prohibited colours = punishable offence.
- ✅ **Preservatives:** Class I (salt, sugar, vinegar) and Class II (Benzoates 210/211, Sorbates 200/202, Sulphites 220/223, Nitrites 249/250). Limits in **mg/kg** vary by food category.
- ✅ **Antioxidants:** Natural (Ascorbic acid 300, Tocopherol 307) and Synthetic (BHA 320, BHT 321, TBHQ 319). Total cap in oils = **200 mg/kg**.
- ✅ **Emulsifiers:** Lecithin 322, Mono-/Di-glycerides 471, Polysorbates 432–436. Selected by **HLB** (Griffin/Davies method).
- ✅ **Stabilisers:** Pectin 440, Agar 406, Carrageenan 407, Xanthan Gum 415, Sodium CMC 466.
- ✅ **Other classes:** Acidulants (Citric 330, Acetic 260), Anticaking (Silicon dioxide 551), Flavour enhancers (MSG 621), Sweeteners (Saccharin 954, Aspartame 951).
- ✅ **HLB ranges:** W/O = 0–6; O/W = 8–18.
- ✅ **Permitted SO₂ in fruit juice (reconstituted)** = 100 mg/kg; in dried fruits = 2000 mg/kg.
- ✅ **Additives must be declared on the label** with class name and INS number.
- ✅ **Statutory body:** FSSAI (under FSS Act, 2006); **Scientific Panels** recommend additives; **CAC** + **JECFA** set international ADI.
- ✅ **Section 26 of FSS Act, 2006:** Use of non-permitted additive → **criminal offence**, penalty up to **₹10 lakh and imprisonment**.
- ✅ **Schedules IV–IX** of the 2011 Regulations list all permitted additives, food categories, and limits.
- ✅ **GMP = "Good Manufacturing Practice"** — the lowest quantity needed to achieve the technological function, not the maximum.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SPS and TBT Agreement, Export & Import Laws and Regulations, WHO/FAO Expert Bodies (JECFA/JEMRA/JMPR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT742 - FOOD LAWS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SPS and TBT Agreement, Export & Import Laws and Regulations, WHO/FAO Expert Bodies (JECFA/JEMRA/JMPR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS Agreement)

### Formal Definition
The **SPS Agreement** is a World Trade Organization (WTO) treaty, formally adopted on **1 January 1995** as part of the Marrakesh Agreement, which sets out the basic rules governing how governments of member nations can apply food safety and animal/plant health measures to international trade. It is codified under Annex 1A of the **Marrakesh Agreement Establishing the WTO**, and is referenced under GATT Articles **XX(b)** and **GATT Article XXI**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The SPS Agreement explicitly recognizes the standards established by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** for food safety, the **International Plant Protection Convention (IPPC)** for plant health, and the **World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH, formerly OIE)** for animal health as the international benchmark reference points.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a massive international airport. Every country is a "gate." The SPS Agreement is essentially the **security checkpoint rulebook**. It says: "Yes, you can screen passengers (food imports) for safety, but you must use internationally recognized scanners (standards), not invent bizarre rules to block competitors." Without this rulebook, Country A could falsely claim "your mangoes are radioactive" just to keep Country B's cheaper mangoes out.

---

## 1.2 The Agreement on Technical Barriers to Trade (TBT Agreement)

### Formal Definition
The **TBT Agreement** is a WTO multilateral treaty that ensures technical regulations, standards, and conformity assessment procedures applied to goods (including food) do not create **unnecessary obstacles to international trade**. It applies to all products, including industrial and agricultural goods, and covers measures beyond the scope of SPS — such as **labelling requirements, packaging, quality specifications, and terminology**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** SPS protects humans, animals, and plants from *biological* and *chemical* risks (pests, diseases, additives, contaminants). TBT protects against *non-safety* technical barriers (size, shape, label language, packaging material). Food labelling is technically a TBT matter; pesticide residue limits are SPS.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
TBT is the **language and dress code inspector** at the airport. Even if your mangoes are perfectly safe (SPS-approved), TBT inspectors check if your packaging says "Mango" in the correct language, in the right font size, with the correct net weight in metric units. They don't care about disease — they care about whether the product *looks, feels, and is described* according to global rules.

---

## 1.3 Export and Import Laws and Regulations in the Food Sector

### Formal Definition
**Export laws** govern the conditions under which food products may legally leave a country (customs, phytosanitary certificates, quality standards of the importer). **Import laws** govern the conditions under which food products may legally enter a country (tariffs, sanitary controls, labelling, permissible additives). In India, these are administered by multiple authorities: **FSSAI** (Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006), **APEDA** (Agricultural & Processed Food Products Export Development Authority Act, 1985), **EIC** (Export Inspection Council), **Plant Quarantine Order 2003**, and **Customs Act, 1962**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Export/Import laws are the **passport and visa system** for food. To leave (export), your product needs an "exit visa" (Phytosanitary Certificate from the exporting country). To enter (import), your product needs an "entry visa" granted by the destination's food safety authority (e.g., **USFDA**, **EFSA** in Europe, **FSSAI** in India).

---

## 1.4 WHO/FAO Expert Bodies — JECFA, JEMRA, JMPR

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** is the joint FAO/WHO body responsible for developing international food standards. To produce scientific advice for Codex, three expert bodies operate as independent scientific committees.

### 1.4.1 JECFA — Joint FAO/WHO Expert Committee on Food Additives
- **Established:** **1956**
- **Mandate:** Evaluates the safety of **food additives, contaminants, naturally occurring toxicants, and residues of veterinary drugs** in food.
- **Key Output:** Sets the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** for additives and the **Tolerable Daily/Weekly Intake (TDI/TWI)** for contaminants. Also establishes **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)** for veterinary drugs.
- **Analogy:** JECFA is the **pharmacist of the global food system** — it tests every chemical added to food and decides how much is "medicinally safe" per day.

### 1.4.2 JEMRA — Joint FAO/WHO Expert Meetings on Microbiological Risk Assessment
- **Established:** **2000**
- **Mandate:** Develops **scientific advice on microbiological hazards** in food (e.g., *Salmonella*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Campylobacter*). Produces **risk assessments** and **risk management guidance**.
- **Analogy:** JEMRA is the **epidemiologist of the food world** — it traces invisible biological threats and predicts outbreaks before they happen.

### 1.4.3 JMPR — Joint Meeting on Pesticide Residues
- **Established:** **1963**
- **Mandate:** Reviews residue data and evaluates the safety of **pesticide residues** in food. Produces **Acceptable Daily Intakes (ADIs)** for pesticides and recommends **Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs)**.
- **Analogy:** JMPR is the **toxicologist with a magnifying glass on crops** — it checks what is left behind on fruits and vegetables after spraying and decides how much residue is acceptable.

### Standard Metrics & Constants
- **ADI Formula Basis:** Usually expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day**.
- **Reference Body Weight for ADI Calculations:** **60 kg** (standard adult used by WHO/FAO).
- **Safety Factor in JECFA/JMPR:** Typically **100** (10× for animal-to-human interspecies variation × 10× for human inter-individual variation).
- **Codex MRL Default:** Expressed in **mg/kg** of the food commodity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The SPS Agreement — Foundational Architecture

### 2.1.1 The Three Core Pillars of SPS

**Pillar 1: Scientific Justification**
- Measures must be **based on scientific principles** and must not be maintained without sufficient scientific evidence (Article 2.2).
- Risk assessment must be conducted using **scientifically validated methods** (Article 5).

**Pillar 2: Harmonization with International Standards**
- Member countries are **strongly encouraged** to base their national SPS measures on international standards (Codex, IPPC, WOAH).
- If a national measure *conforms* to the international standard, it is **presumed to be consistent** with SPS obligations.

**Pillar 3: Equivalence and Recognition**
- WTO members must accept the SPS measures of other members as **equivalent**, even if they differ from their own, provided the importing country's level of protection is achieved (Article 4).

### 2.1.2 The SPS Risk Assessment Procedure

The SPS risk assessment follows a four-step structured framework:

1. **Hazard Identification** — Identify the biological, chemical, or physical agent capable of causing harm.
2. **Hazard Characterization** — Qualitatively or quantitatively describe the adverse effects.
3. **Exposure Assessment** — Estimate the actual intake by humans, animals, or plants.
4. **Risk Characterization** — Combine hazard characterization with exposure assessment to estimate the probability and severity of harm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Levels of Protection (ALOP):** The SPS Agreement allows each country to set its own **Appropriate Level of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Protection (ALOP)** — but this level must be **consistent** and not used as a disguised trade barrier.

### 2.1.3 Special and Differential Treatment
Developing countries (including **India as a developing nation**) are entitled to longer time-frames for compliance with new SPS measures and may receive technical assistance from developed member countries.

---

## 2.2 The TBT Agreement — Foundational Architecture

### 2.2.1 Key Provisions

**Legitimate Objectives (Article 2.2):** TBT measures must pursue a legitimate objective, which explicitly includes:
- **National security**
- **Prevention of deceptive practices**
- **Protection of human health or safety**
- **Protection of animal or plant life or health**
- **Protection of the environment**

**Six Principles of TBT:**
1. **Non-discrimination** (Most-Favoured-Nation and National Treatment).
2. **Transparency** (Notification of draft regulations to WTO).
3. **Necessity** (Measures should not be more trade-restrictive than necessary).
4. **Proportionality**.
5. **Harmonization** with international standards (ISO, IEC, Codex for food labelling).
6. **Conformity Assessment Acceptance**.

### 2.2.2 Code of Good Practice (Annex 3 of TBT)
Voluntary code applying to **standardizing bodies** (both governmental and non-governmental) preparing voluntary standards. It mandates that standards be:
- Transparent
- Open to consultation
- Based on consensus
- Re-evaluated periodically

---

## 2.3 Export & Import Laws — The Indian Framework

### 2.3.1 Export Regulatory Architecture (India)

| Authority | Legal Basis | Function |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **DGFT** (Directorate General of Foreign Trade) | Foreign Trade (Development & Regulation) Act, 1992 | Issues IEC code, export licenses |
| **APEDA** | APEDA Act, 1985 | Promotes export of scheduled products (fruits, vegetables, meat) |
| **EIC** (Export Inspection Council) | Export (Quality Control and Inspection) Act, 1963 | Pre-shipment inspection, certification |
| **Plant Quarantine** | Plant Quarantine Order, 2003 | Phytosanitary certificates for export |
| **FSSAI** | FSS Act, 2006 | Food safety standards for all food including exports |

### 2.3.2 Import Regulatory Architecture (India)

| Authority | Legal Basis | Function |
|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **FSSAI** | FSS Act, 2006 (Chapter VIII) | Licensing of importers, food safety clearance |
| **Customs** | Customs Act, 1962 | Tariff classification, clearance at port |
| **Plant Quarantine** | Plant Quarantine Order, 2003 | Import release of plant-origin foods |
| **Animal Quarantine** | Livestock Importation Act, 1898 | Animal-origin food imports |
| **Legal Metrology** | Legal Metrology (Packaged Commodities) Rules, 2011 | Net quantity, declarations on imported packaged food |

### 2.3.3 FSSAI Import Clearance Process
- Importer obtains **FSSAI License** under **Chapter II / Chapter III of FSS Act, 2006**.
- File **Bill of Entry** with customs.
- Food sample drawn by **Authorised Officer (AO)**.
- Sample sent to **FSSAI Notified Laboratory**.
- **No Objection Certificate (NOC)** or rejection issued.

---

## 2.4 JECFA — The Detailed Operating Model

### 2.4.1 Evaluation Principles
- Uses the **No-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level (NOAEL)** from long-term animal studies.
- Applies **safety factors** to extrapolate to humans.
- For contaminants, calculates the **margin of exposure (MOE)** relative to a **Benchmark Dose Lower Confidence Limit (BMDL)**.

### 2.4.2 The ADI Derivation Logic (Stepwise)

1. Conduct long-term toxicology studies in animals (typically **2 species**, rat and dog).
2. Identify the **NOAEL** — the highest dose at which no adverse effect is observed.
3. Apply a **safety factor (SF)** — default **100** (10× intra-species × 10× inter-species).
4. Calculate: **ADI = NOAEL / SF**.

### 2.4.3 Special JECFA Categories
- **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI):** For intentional additives with a defined threshold.
- **Tolerable Daily Intake (TDI):** For unavoidable contaminants (lead, cadmium, mercury).
- **Tolerable Weekly Intake (TWI):** For bioaccumulative contaminants (used in earlier JECFA practice for lead — later revised to a TDI in 2023 for lead; current TWI for cadmium is **2.5 µg/kg bw/week**).

---

## 2.5 JEMRA — The Detailed Operating Model

### 2.5.1 JEMRA Risk Assessment Framework
JEMRA applies a structured **Microbiological Risk Assessment (MRA)** process, mirroring the SPS risk model:

1. **Hazard Identification** — e.g., *Salmonella* spp. in poultry.
2. **Hazard Characterization** — Dose-response relationship, severity of illness.
3. **Exposure Assessment** — From farm to fork; prevalence × concentration × consumption.
4. **Risk Characterization** — Probability of illness per serving or per year.

### 2.5.2 Outputs of JEMRA
- **Risk Assessments** (full qualitative/quantitative).
- **Risk Profiles** (preliminary short reports).
- **Guidelines for Risk Management** (e.g., control measures for *Listeria* in ready-to-eat foods).
- **Meeting Reports** (e.g., *Salmonella* in eggs, *Campylobacter* in broiler meat).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> JEMRA does **not** set MRLs or ADIs — that is JECFA's role. JEMRA focuses on **biological hazards**, JECFA on **chemical hazards**.

---

## 2.6 JMPR — The Detailed Operating Model

### 2.6.1 JMPR Structure
JMPR consists of:
- **WHO Core Assessment Group** — Reviews toxicological data.
- **FAO Panel of Experts** — Reviews residue data from supervised field trials.

### 2.6.2 JMPR Outputs
- **ADI for the pesticide** (based on toxicology).
- **Acute Reference Dose (ARfD)** — for single-day exposure, established since **1999** for pesticides with acute toxicity concerns.
- **MRLs** — Maximum Residue Limits in mg/kg for specific crop-pesticide combinations.
- **Estimated Maximum Daily Intake (EMDI)** and **International Estimated Daily Intake (IEDI)** — to verify ADI is not exceeded.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Standard Value / Unit** | **Body** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acceptable Daily Intake | $ADI$ | $ADI = \dfrac{NOAEL}{SF}$ | mg/kg bw/day | JECFA / JMPR |
| Safety Factor | $SF$ | $SF = SF_{inter} \times SF_{intra}$ | $10 \times 10 = \mathbf{100}$ | JECFA / JMPR |
| Hazard Quotient | $HQ$ | $HQ = \dfrac{EDI}{ADI}$ | dimensionless; HQ > 1 = concern | JECFA / JMPR |
| Estimated Daily Intake | $EDI$ | $EDI = \dfrac{C \times F}{BW}$ | mg/kg bw/day | All |
| International Estimated Daily Intake | $IEDI$ | $IEDI = \sum \dfrac{MRL \times F}{BW}$ | mg/kg bw/day | JMPR |
| Margin of Exposure | $MOE$ | $MOE = \dfrac{BMDL}{Estimated Human Exposure}$ | dimensionless; > 10,000 = low concern | JECFA |
| Theoretical Maximum Daily Intake | $TMDI$ | $TMDI = \sum \dfrac{MRL \times F}{BW}$ | mg/kg bw/day | JMPR |
| Highest Residue Unit | $HR$ | Empirical from field trials | mg/kg | JMPR |
| Supervised Trials Median Residue | $STMR$ | Median of supervised field trial residues | mg/kg | JMPR |

> **Where:** $C$ = concentration of substance in food (mg/kg); $F$ = daily food consumption (kg/day); $BW$ = body weight (typically **60 kg** under WHO/FAO convention).

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering / Regulatory Utility

In the food industry, these frameworks are the foundation of:
- **International food trade** (every consignment crossing a border is governed by SPS/TBT).
- **Setting national food safety standards** (Codex standards are the *default* under SPS).
- **Designing risk-based inspection systems** (JEMRA-inspired Hazard Analysis Critical Control Point — **HACCP**).
- **Export certification of Indian spices, basmati rice, processed foods** to EU, US, Japan.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Calculation of Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)

This is the most important calculation in JECFA/JMPR work and is a high-frequency KTU question.

### Problem Statement
A chronic toxicity study in rats on a new food additive identifies a **No-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level (NOAEL) of 200 mg/kg bw/day**. Calculate the Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) of this additive.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the available toxicological endpoint.**
The chronic study identifies the highest dose at which no statistically or biologically significant adverse effect is observed in test animals.

$$
NOAEL = 200 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}
$$

**Step 2: Identify the uncertainty factors required.**
- $SF_{inter}$ = Interspecies uncertainty factor (animal to human) = $\mathbf{10}$
- $SF_{intra}$ = Intraspecies (human-to-human variability) uncertainty factor = $\mathbf{10}$

**Step 3: Compute the composite safety factor.**

$$
SF = SF_{inter} \times SF_{intra}
$$

$$
SF = 10 \times 10 = \mathbf{100}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the ADI formula.**

$$
ADI = \dfrac{NOAEL}{SF}
$$

$$
ADI = \dfrac{200 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}}{100}
$$

$$
\boxed{ADI = 2.0 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}}
$$

**Step 5: Translate into an absolute daily intake for a standard adult (BW = 60 kg).**

$$
ADI_{absolute} = ADI \times BW
$$

$$
ADI_{absolute} = 2.0 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day} \times 60 \ \text{kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{ADI_{absolute} = 120 \ \text{mg/day}}
$$

**Step 6: Real-world application — verify against Estimated Daily Intake (EDI).**
Suppose a person consumes **300 g/day** of a food containing the additive at the **Maximum Permitted Level of 50 mg/kg**.

$$
EDI = \dfrac{C \times F}{BW}
$$

$$
EDI = \dfrac{50 \ \text{mg/kg} \times 0.300 \ \text{kg/day}}{60 \ \text{kg}}
$$

$$
EDI = \dfrac{15}{60} = 0.25 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the Hazard Quotient.**

$$
HQ = \dfrac{EDI}{ADI}
$$

$$
HQ = \dfrac{0.25}{2.0} = \mathbf{0.125}
$$

Since $HQ < 1$, the additive is **safe** under the assumed consumption pattern.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: International Estimated Daily Intake (IEDI) of a Pesticide

### Problem Statement
The JMPR establishes an MRL of **2 mg/kg** for a pesticide (P) in apples and **5 mg/kg** in wheat. The average daily consumption of apples is **100 g/day** and of wheat-based products is **250 g/day** in a 60-kg adult population. The ADI of the pesticide is **0.05 mg/kg bw/day**. Verify whether dietary exposure exceeds the ADI.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the IEDI formula.**

$$
IEDI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \dfrac{MRL_i \times F_i}{BW} \right)
$$

**Step 2: Calculate apple contribution.**

$$
IEDI_{apple} = \dfrac{2 \ \text{mg/kg} \times 0.100 \ \text{kg/day}}{60 \ \text{kg}} = \dfrac{0.20}{60} = 0.00333 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate wheat contribution.**

$$
IEDI_{wheat} = \dfrac{5 \ \text{mg/kg} \times 0.250 \ \text{kg/day}}{60 \ \text{kg}} = \dfrac{1.25}{60} = 0.02083 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}
$$

**Step 4: Sum the contributions.**

$$
IEDI_{total} = 0.00333 + 0.02083 = 0.02417 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with ADI.**

$$
HQ = \dfrac{IEDI_{total}}{ADI} = \dfrac{0.02417}{0.05} = \mathbf{0.483}
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation.**

$$
HQ = 0.483 < 1.0
$$

The dietary exposure to pesticide P is **below the ADI** under the assumed consumption and residue patterns. The MRLs are considered **acceptable** by the JMPR.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Margin of Exposure (MOE) for a Contaminant

### Problem Statement
A JECFA evaluation of a contaminant determines a **BMDL$_{10}$ of 1.5 mg/kg bw/day**. The estimated human exposure in a regional population is **0.00005 mg/kg bw/day**. Calculate the Margin of Exposure and interpret.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the MOE formula.**

$$
MOE = \dfrac{BMDL_{10}}{\text{Estimated Human Exposure}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
MOE = \dfrac{1.5}{0.00005}
$$

$$
\boxed{MOE = 30{,}000}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation using WHO/EFSA guidelines.**

- $MOE \geq 10{,}000$ → **Low health concern**.
- $MOE$ between $1{,}000$ and $10{,}000$ → **Possible health concern**.
- $MOE < 1{,}000$ → **Clear health concern**.

Since $MOE = 30{,}000 \geq 10{,}000$, the contaminant exposure is of **low health concern**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Roles of JECFA, JEMRA, and JMPR

| **Feature** | **JECFA** | **JEMRA** | **JMPR** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Year Established** | 1956 | 2000 | 1963 |
| **Parent Bodies** | Joint FAO/WHO | Joint FAO/WHO | Joint FAO/WHO |
| **Scope** | Food additives, contaminants, veterinary drug residues | Microbiological hazards | Pesticide residues |
| **Hazard Type** | Chemical | Biological | Chemical |
| **Key Output 1** | ADI / TDI / TWI | Risk Assessment Reports | ADI, ARfD |
| **Key Output 2** | MRLs (veterinary drugs) | Risk Management Guidelines | MRLs (pesticides) |
| **Decision Metric** | NOAEL/SF | Dose-response curves | NOAEL/SF |
| **Reference Body Weight** | 60 kg adult | 60 kg adult | 60 kg adult |
| **Default Safety Factor** | 100 | Not applicable (qualitative) | 100 |
| **Codex Linkage** | Codex Committee on Food Additives (CCFA), CCFH | Codex Committee on Food Hygiene (CCFH) | Codex Committee on Pesticide Residues (CCPR) |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: SPS vs. TBT — Critical Distinctions

| **Parameter** | **SPS Agreement** | **TBT Agreement** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Food safety, animal/plant health, pests, diseases | Labelling, packaging, quality, terminology |
| **Trigger of Measure** | Biological / chemical risk to life/health | Consumer protection, prevention of deception |
| **International Reference Body** | Codex, IPPC, WOAH | ISO, IEC, ITU |
| **Risk Assessment Requirement** | Mandatory | Not mandatory (only for safety-related TBT) |
| **Precautionary Principle** | Explicitly allowed (Article 5.7) | Implicitly allowed |
| **Coverage of Food Labelling** | Limited to food safety (e.g., allergens via CCFL) | Comprehensive (nutrition, ingredients, date marking) |
| **Transparency Obligation** | Notification to WTO SPS Committee | Notification to WTO TBT Committee |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Answer in approximately **80–100 words**. Each question carries **3 marks**. No choice in Part A.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Discuss the role of the SPS Agreement in international food trade."** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation)
**Definition (1 mark):** The Agreement on the Application of Sanitary and Phytosanitary Measures (SPS) came into force on **1 January 1995** under the WTO framework. It governs how member countries apply food safety, animal, and plant health measures to international trade.

**Key Roles (2 marks):**
1. **Prevents Unjustified Trade Barriers:** Requires SPS measures to be scientifically justified, preventing disguised protectionism.
2. **Promotes Harmonization:** Recognizes **Codex Alimentarius, IPPC, and WOAH** standards as the international benchmark.
3. **Ensures Transparency:** Members must notify WTO of new SPS measures affecting trade.
4. **Allows Risk-Based Decision-Making:** Introduces structured **risk assessment** (hazard ID, characterization, exposure, characterization).

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between JECFA, JEMRA, and JMPR."** *(3 Marks)*

### Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation)
**Common Base (1 mark):** All three are **Joint FAO/WHO expert bodies** that provide independent scientific advice to the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**.

**Differentiation Table (2 marks):**

| **JECFA** | **JEMRA** | **JMPR** |
|---|---|---|
| Established **1956** | Established **2000** | Established **1963** |
| Evaluates **food additives, contaminants, veterinary drug residues** | Evaluates **microbiological hazards** | Evaluates **pesticide residues** |
| Establishes **ADI, TDI, MRLs** | Produces **risk assessment reports** | Establishes **ADI, ARfD, MRLs** |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Answer any **one full question** from the choice provided. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total **14 marks**.

---

### CHOICE 1 — QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principles and objectives of the SPS Agreement**. Discuss how the **SPS Agreement influences food import-export regulations** in India. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **structure, scope, and outputs of JECFA**. With a suitable example, derive the **Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI)** of a food additive. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Part 1: Principles of SPS Agreement (3 Marks)**
1. **Scientific Justification** — All SPS measures must be based on scientific principles and risk assessment.
2. **Harmonization** — Measures should be based on **Codex, IPPC, and WOAH** standards.
3. **Equivalence** — Member countries must accept equivalent measures from trading partners (Article 4).
4. **Transparency** — Mandatory notification to the **WTO SPS Committee** of new measures.
5. **Special and Differential Treatment** — Developing countries (like India) get longer compliance windows.
6. **Precautionary Principle (Article 5.7)** — Provisional measures allowed when scientific evidence is insufficient.

**Part 2: Objectives (2 Marks)**
- Minimize **negative trade effects** of SPS measures.
- Protect human, animal, and plant **life and health** from pests, diseases, and food-borne risks.
- Establish a **multilateral framework of rules** for SPS measures.

**Part 3: Influence on Indian Food Import-Export (2 Marks)**
- India's **FSSAI standards** are aligned with **Codex Alimentarius** for mutual recognition.
- **Phytosanitary certificates** issued by India's **Plant Quarantine** authority are SPS-compliant.
- India has been involved in **WTO SPS dispute cases** (e.g., EC-Hormones case as observer of the dispute).
- Exporters must comply with the **SPS requirements of importing countries** (e.g., aflatoxin limits for groundnut exports to EU).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Part 1: Structure and Scope of JECFA (3 Marks)**
- **Established:** 1956 under joint sponsorship of **FAO and WHO**.
- **Mandate:** Evaluates the safety of **food additives, contaminants, naturally occurring toxicants, and veterinary drug residues** in food.
- **Secretariat:** Hosted alternately by **WHO (Geneva)** and **FAO (Rome)**.
- **Outputs:** Publishes **ADI values** for additives, **TDI/TWI** for contaminants, and **MRLs** for veterinary drugs.

**Part 2: Derivation of ADI with Example (4 Marks)**

Given:
- NOAEL from chronic rat study = $250$ mg/kg bw/day
- Safety Factor (SF) = $10 \times 10 = 100$

**Step 1: Identify NOAEL**

$$
NOAEL = 250 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}
$$

**Step 2: Apply safety factor**

$$
SF = 100
$$

**Step 3: Calculate ADI**

$$
ADI = \dfrac{NOAEL}{SF} = \dfrac{250}{100}
$$

$$
\boxed{ADI = 2.5 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}}
$$

**Step 4: Absolute daily intake for 60 kg adult**

$$
ADI_{absolute} = 2.5 \times 60 = 150 \ \text{mg/day}
$$

**Step 5: Safety verification using Hazard Quotient**

If the food contains the additive at **40 mg/kg** and a person consumes **200 g/day**:

$$
EDI = \dfrac{40 \times 0.200}{60} = 0.133 \ \text{mg/kg bw/day}
$$

$$
HQ = \dfrac{0.133}{2.5} = 0.053 < 1 \quad \text{(Safe)}
$$

---

### CHOICE 1 — QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **key differences between SPS and TBT Agreements** with suitable examples from the food sector. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **mandate and risk assessment methodology of JEMRA**. Discuss its contribution to **Codex standards for food hygiene**. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Part 1: Conceptual Difference (2 Marks)**
- **SPS** addresses **food safety, animal, and plant health** from biological/chemical risks.
- **TBT** addresses **technical specifications, labelling, packaging, and quality** that may create trade barriers.

**Part 2: Comparative Table (3 Marks)**

| **Aspect** | **SPS** | **TBT** |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Food safety / animal & plant health | Technical regulations / labelling |
| Examples | MRL of pesticides, microbial limits, quarantine | "Best before" date marking, ingredient declaration, metric net weight |
| Standard Bodies | Codex, IPPC, WOAH | ISO, IEC, ITU |
| Risk Assessment | Mandatory | Only for safety-related TBT |

**Part 3: Food Sector Examples (2 Marks)**
- **SPS Example:** EU rejecting Indian mangoes in 2014 due to fruit fly concerns — phytosanitary measure.
- **TBT Example:** EU Regulation No. 1169/2011 on **Food Information to Consumers** (FIC) — mandates allergen labelling, nutrition declaration — purely a TBT measure.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Part 1: Mandate of JEMRA (2 Marks)**
- **Established in 2000** as a joint FAO/WHO initiative.
- Conducts **Microbiological Risk Assessment (MRA)** for pathogens in food (*Salmonella*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Campylobacter*, *E. coli* O157:H7, *Cronobacter*, norovirus).
- Provides **scientific advice** to the **Codex Committee on Food Hygiene (CCFH)**.

**Part 2: Risk Assessment Methodology (3 Marks)**
The four-step MRA process:
1. **Hazard Identification** — e.g., *Listeria monocytogenes* in ready-to-eat (RTE) meats.
2. **Hazard Characterization** — Dose-response curve: probability of listeriosis per cell ingested.
3. **Exposure Assessment** — Frequency and concentration in RTE foods × consumption pattern.
4. **Risk Characterization** — Predicted cases of illness per 100,000 population per year.

**Part 3: Contribution to Codex Standards (2 Marks)**
- *Salmonella* in Eggs and Broiler Chickens (2002, revised 2009).
- *Listeria monocytogenes* in RTE Foods (2002, updated 2024).
- *Campylobacter* in Broiler Chickens (2008, updated 2024).
- These are used by the **Codex Code of Hygienic Practice** and **Codex Guidelines for the Control of Pathogens**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students confuse **JECFA** (chemical) with **JEMRA** (microbiological) — the KTU examiner expects **explicit identification of the hazard type**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Failing to mention the **default body weight of 60 kg** in ADI calculations results in a **2-mark deduction**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not distinguishing between **MRL (Maximum Residue Limit, mg/kg)** and **ADI (mg/kg bw/day)** — these are **different units and different concepts**; mixing them loses **3 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Treating SPS and TBT as synonyms — KTU frequently tests this distinction for **direct 3–5 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Omitting the **Codex Alimentarius Commission's role** as the central node linking FAO/WHO expert bodies with WTO SPS — this is a **high-frequency valuation trap**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### **WTO Agreements**
- ✅ SPS Agreement (1995) — food safety, animal and plant health; references **Codex, IPPC, WOAH**.
- ✅ TBT Agreement (1995) — technical regulations, labelling, packaging; references **ISO, IEC**.
- ✅ Both operate under the WTO framework; dispute settlement through **DSB**.
- ✅ Precautionary principle is **explicit in SPS Article 5.7**, implicit in TBT.

### **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)**
- ✅ Joint FAO/WHO body established in **1963**.
- ✅ Coordinates international food standards through specialized **Codex Committees**.
- ✅ Standards are the **reference point** for SPS measures globally.

### **JECFA — Key Points**
- ✅ Established **1956**; evaluates food **additives, contaminants, veterinary drug residues**.
- ✅ Sets **ADI, TDI, TWI, MRLs** for veterinary drugs.
- ✅ Default safety factor = **100**; standard body weight = **60 kg**.
- ✅ Formula: $ADI = NOAEL / 100$.

### **JEMRA — Key Points**
- ✅ Established **2000**; evaluates **microbiological** hazards.
- ✅ Conducts **4-step MRA** — Hazard ID → Characterization → Exposure → Risk Characterization.
- ✅ Does NOT set MRLs or ADIs (chemical-only outputs are JECFA's domain).
- ✅ Advises **CCFH** on food hygiene standards.

### **JMPR — Key Points**
- ✅ Established **1963**; evaluates **pesticide residues**.
- ✅ Sets **ADI, ARfD, MRLs** for pesticides.
- ✅ Uses **NOAEL/SF = 100** and **STMR/HR** from field trials.
- ✅ Advises **CCPR** (Codex Committee on Pesticide Residues).

### **Indian Regulatory Framework — Key Points**
- ✅ **FSSAI** — apex food safety regulator under **FSS Act, 2006**.
- ✅ **APEDA** — export promotion for scheduled agricultural products (1985 Act).
- ✅ **EIC** — pre-shipment inspection and certification.
- ✅ **Plant Quarantine Order, 2003** — phytosanitary certificates.
- ✅ **Customs Act, 1962** — import clearance at ports.

### **Critical Exam Formulas**
- ✅ $ADI = NOAEL / SF$ — must use $SF = 100$ by default.
- ✅ $EDI = (C \times F) / BW$ — verify $HQ = EDI / ADI < 1$.
- ✅ $IEDI = \sum (MRL_i \times F_i) / BW$.
- ✅ $MOE = BMDL / Human Exposure$; safe if $MOE \geq 10{,}000$.
- ✅ Standard adult body weight = **60 kg** (WHO/FAO).

### **High-Yield Comparison Traps (KTU Favourites)**
- ✅ **SPS vs. TBT** — Question-worthy as a direct sub-part.
- ✅ **JECFA vs. JEMRA vs. JMPR** — Differentiate by hazard type (chemical / biological) and year of establishment.
- ✅ **NOAEL vs. LOAEL vs. BMDL** — Understand which safety metric is used where.
- ✅ **ADI vs. ARfD vs. TDI vs. TWI** — Each has a unique application context.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — PEFTT742 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
