```mermaid
    PlanD --> KitE[Kitchen Receives Indent]
    KitE --> PrepF[Food Preparation under HACCP]
    PrepF --> QC1[Temperature Check Greater Than 63 Degree Celsius]
    QC1 --> PlatG[Plating and Traying with Color Coded Tags]
    PlatG --> TransH[Insulated Trolley Transport]
    TransH --> WardI[Distribution to Patient Bedside]
    WardI --> Feed[J[Patient Consumption and Feedback]]
    Feed --> KPIK[Monthly Diet Satisfaction Index Calculation]
    KPIK --> CQI[Continuous Quality Improvement Loop]
    CQI --> DietC
```

### 4.3 NABH Accreditation Lifecycle Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Application to NABH] --> A2[Document Review]
    A2 --> A3[Pre Assessment Visit]
    A3 --> A4[Final Assessment by Assessor Team]
    A4 --> A5{Compliance Status}
    A5 --> A6[Accreditation Granted Validity 3 Years]
    A5 --> A7[Improvement Required Re Submission in 3 Months]
    A7 --> A4
    A6 --> A8[Annual Surveillance Audit]
    A8 --> A9[Re Assessment Before 3 Year Expiry]
    A9 --> A4
```

### 4.4 HACCP-NABH Integration Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    HAC1[HACCP Principle 1 Hazard Analysis] --> HAC2[HACCP Principle 2 Critical Control Points]
    HAC2 --> HAC3[HACCP Principle 3 Critical Limits]
    HAC3 --> HAC4[HACCP Principle 4 Monitoring Procedures]
    HAC4 --> HAC5[HACCP Principle 5 Corrective Actions]
    HAC5 --> HAC6[HACCP Principle 6 Verification]
    HAC6 --> HAC7[HACCP Principle 7 Record Keeping]
    HAC1 -.NABH Overlay.-> NAB1[FMS Facility Safety Standards]
    HAC2 -.NABH Overlay.-> NAB2[COP Care of Patient Standards]
    HAC4 -.NABH Overlay.-> NAB3[CQI Continuous Quality Improvement]
    HAC7 -.NABH Overlay.-> NAB4[Documentation and Audit Trail]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define NABH. Mention its parent body and the year of establishment.**

**Model Answer:**

> **NABH (National Accreditation Board for Hospitals and Healthcare Enterprises)** is a constituent board of the **Quality Council of India (QCI)**, established in **2005**. It functions as the apex national body for accreditation of healthcare organizations, with a dedicated framework for evaluating **hospital food service, dietary care, and kitchen safety** against defined standards of quality, hygiene, and patient-centred nutrition. The 5th edition of NABH Hospital Standards (2020) explicitly incorporates chapters on **Facility Management and Safety (FMS)** and **Care of Patients (COP)** that govern kitchen operations, therapeutic diets, and food handler hygiene.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any three food-safety parameters mandated by NABH for hospital kitchens.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cold storage temperature** must be maintained at $\leq 5 \degree\text{C}$ at all times, logged **twice daily** on a temperature chart.
2. **Hot-holding temperature** for cooked food must be $\geq 63 \degree\text{C}$; food in the danger zone ($5 \degree\text{C}$ to $63 \degree\text{C}$) must not exceed 2 hours.
3. **Food handler medical examination** must be conducted **every 6 months** with records maintained, and **handwash stations** must be available at every entry and exit of the kitchen as per HIC standards.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q. A (a)** Explain the objectives, scope, and significance of NABH accreditation with special reference to hospital food services. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Objectives of NABH:**
1. To establish a uniform **national benchmark** for quality in healthcare delivery.
2. To **accredit hospitals and healthcare providers** through objective evaluation.
3. To **promote patient safety** through standardized clinical and supportive services.
4. To **align Indian healthcare** with international standards (ISQua).

**Scope with reference to food services:**
- Evaluation of **kitchen infrastructure** (SS 304 surfaces, exhaust systems, drainage).
- Assessment of **dietary workflow** — from physician's diet order to bedside delivery.
- Verification of **therapeutic nutrition planning** by qualified dietitians.
- Audit of **food safety protocols** aligned with HACCP and FSSAI Schedule 4.
- Monitoring of **patient satisfaction and feedback** on diet quality.

**Significance:**
- Enhances **patient trust** in hospital food safety.
- **Mandatory** for empanelment under PMJAY, CGHS, ECHS, and major insurance providers.
- Reduces **hospital-acquired foodborne infections (HAFIs)**.
- Encourages **continuous quality improvement** through documented SOPs and KPIs.

**Q. A (b)** Discuss the NABH standards for **dietary services and kitchen safety** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

NABH has integrated food service requirements under three principal chapters:

**(i) Care of Patients (COP):** Mandates that a **qualified Registered Dietitian** must assess every admitted patient within **24 hours** of admission. Diet plans must be **individualized** (e.g., diabetic, renal, low-sodium, high-protein) and **documented** in the patient record. The plan must be **reassessed weekly** or upon change in clinical condition.

**(ii) Facility Management & Safety (FMS):** The kitchen must have:
- **Epoxy-coated or vitrified tile flooring** with gradient for drainage.
- **SS 304 grade stainless steel** work tables and storage racks.
- **Fire suppression systems** and **LPG leak detectors**.
- **Adequate ventilation** with exhaust fans and air curtains to prevent pest entry.

**(iii) Hospital Infection Control (HIC):** Food handlers must undergo:
- **Pre-employment and 6-monthly medical examination** including stool culture.
- **Hand hygiene training** with documented compliance.
- Use of **gloves, headgear, aprons, and closed footwear** while handling food.

**Additional requirements:**
- **Sample retention** of every meal for $\geq 72$ hours for microbiological analysis.
- **Monthly internal audits** and **annual external surveillance** by NABH assessors.
- **Diet satisfaction score** $S_{diet} \geq 80\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> - Students often write only about **FSSAI** while ignoring **NABH-specific standards** like the $\geq 80\%$ diet satisfaction KPI, the 72-hour sample retention rule, and the 24-hour dietitian assessment.
> - Confusing **NABH (accreditation body)** with **FSSAI (regulatory body)** is a frequent error. Remember: **FSSAI issues licenses; NABH issues accreditation certificates.**
> - Forgetting to mention the **3-year accreditation cycle** and **annual surveillance** loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. B (a)** Describe the **HACCP-based food safety framework** as required under NABH accreditation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

NABH mandates the implementation of the **seven principles of HACCP** in hospital kitchens:

**Principle 1 — Hazard Analysis:** Identify biological (Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria), chemical (cleaning agents, allergens), and physical (glass, metal) hazards at every stage from procurement to consumption.

**Principle 2 — Critical Control Points (CCPs):** Establish checkpoints such as **cooking temperature ($\geq 74\degree\text{C}$ core)**, **hot-holding ($\geq 63\degree\text{C}$)**, and **cold storage ($\leq 5\degree\text{C}$)**.

**Principle 3 — Critical Limits:** Set measurable limits, e.g., $T_{cold} \leq 5 \degree\text{C}$ and $T_{hot} \geq 63 \degree\text{C}$.

**Principle 4 — Monitoring Procedures:** Use calibrated thermometers, automated data loggers, and **twice-daily temperature logs** maintained for at least **3 years**.

**Principle 5 — Corrective Actions:** Define SOPs for deviations — e.g., discarding food held below $63\degree\text{C}$ for over 2 hours, recalibrating thermometers, and reporting incidents to the Infection Control Committee.

**Principle 6 — Verification:** Conduct **monthly internal audits**, **quarterly microbiological swab testing** of surfaces, and **annual third-party surveillance audits** by NABH assessors.

**Principle 7 — Record-Keeping:** Maintain digital and physical records of temperature logs, supplier bills, food handler medical reports, and patient diet charts for traceability.

**Q. B (b)** Compare the **roles of FSSAI and NABH** in regulating hospital food services in India. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | FSSAI | NABH |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Status** | Statutory regulator under FSS Act, 2006 | Voluntary accreditation body under QCI |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes — license required to operate any food business | No, but de facto required for empanelment |
| **Focus** | Product standards, licensing, enforcement | Process quality, patient-centred care, continuous improvement |
| **Penalty Power** | Yes — fines up to Rs. 10 lakh, prosecution | Loss of accreditation; no criminal liability |
| **Hospital Relevance** | General food safety | Hospital-specific diet, kitchen, infection control |
| **Audit Style** | Inspection-based, punitive | Assessment-based, supportive |
| **Documentation** | License, label, sample | SOPs, KPIs, satisfaction surveys |
| **Recognition** | Indian law | National and international (ISQua) |

**Conclusion:** FSSAI and NABH are **complementary, not competing**. FSSAI ensures baseline legal compliance, while NABH raises the bar to **world-class quality and patient safety** in hospital food services.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> - Writing **HACCP** without naming all **seven principles** forfeits 2 marks.
> - Confusing **NABH (accreditation)** with **FSSAI (regulation)** in the comparison table costs heavy marks.
> - Failing to cite **specific temperatures and time limits** makes the answer vague and loses marks on the **critical limits** sub-question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — NABH**

- **Full form:** National Accreditation Board for Hospitals and Healthcare Enterprises.
- **Parent body:** Quality Council of India (QCI).
- **Year established:** 2005.
- **Standards edition (current):** 5th Edition, 2020.
- **Accreditation validity:** 3 years (with annual surveillance).
- **Cold storage temperature:** $\leq 5 \degree\text{C}$.
- **Hot-holding temperature:** $\geq 63 \degree\text{C}$.
- **Reheat core temperature:** $\geq 74 \degree\text{C}$.
- **Diet satisfaction target:** $\geq 80\%$.
- **Dietitian assessment time:** $\leq 24$ hours of admission.
- **Food handler medical exam:** Every 6 months.
- **Sample retention:** $\geq 72$ hours per meal.
- **Kitchen audit frequency:** Monthly internal; annual external.
- **International alignment:** ISQua (International Society for Quality in Health Care).
- **Key chapters for food:** FMS, COP, HIC, CQI.
- **NABH vs. FSSAI:** NABH = accreditation (quality); FSSAI = regulation (license).
- **HACCP principles:** 7 — must be implemented in all NABH kitchens.
- **Empanelment link:** Mandatory for PMJAY (Ayushman Bharat), CGHS, ECHS.
- **Kitchen material standard:** SS 304 grade stainless steel.
- **Flooring:** Epoxy or vitrified with drainage gradient.
- **PPE for food handlers:** Gloves, headgear, aprons, closed footwear.
- **Danger zone to avoid:** $5 \degree\text{C} \leq T \leq 63 \degree\text{C}$ for cooked food.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NBQP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT742 - FOOD LAWS AND REGULATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NBQP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of NBQP

**NBQP** stands for the **National Bee Board Quality Mark** (also referred to in some literature as the *National Bee Keeping Quality Mark*). It is an officially recognised **third-party quality certification scheme** for honey and other bee-hive products manufactured and/or processed in India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *NBQP is a voluntary, government-backed quality certification mark instituted by the **National Bee Board (NBB)** under the **Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, Government of India**. It assures consumers that certified honey conforms to the **Food Safety and Standards (Food Products Standards and Food Additives) Regulations, 2011**, BIS Standards (IS 4941:1994 for extracted honey), and the specific NBQP parameter sheet issued by NBB.*

### 1.2 Governing Body

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| Operating Authority | **National Bee Board (NBB)** |
| Parent Ministry | Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare (Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare) |
| Headquarters | New Delhi |
| Established | 1993 (revamped Quality Mark scheme launched: **2018**) |
| Applicable Products | **Extracted Honey**, **Beeswax**, **Royal Jelly**, **Bee Pollen**, **Propolis** |
| Type of Mark | **Voluntary** (Third-Party Assured) |

### 1.3 Intuitive / Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of NBQP like a "Hallmark" stamp on gold jewellery.**
> When you buy a gold ring with a **BIS Hallmark**, you know the purity is government-verified, not just claimed by the jeweller. Similarly, when you buy honey bearing the **NBQP logo**, the National Bee Board is publicly guaranteeing that the honey inside the jar has been independently tested and meets stringent quality parameters (moisture, HMF, sugar profile, antibiotic residue, heavy metals, etc.). It is the difference between *trusting the label* and *trusting an audited, traceable quality chain*.

### 1.4 Why NBQP Exists — The Problem It Solves

India is one of the world's largest **honey exporters** (top 5 globally), yet for decades honey was plagued by:

- **Adulteration with sugar syrups** (rice syrup, corn syrup, invert sugar).
- Misrepresentation of floral/geographical origin.
- Presence of **antibiotic residues** (especially chloramphenicol, nitrofurans) leading to export rejections in the EU and US.
- Lack of a unified domestic quality mark to differentiate genuine processors from adulterators.

NBQP was introduced as a **trust signal** to protect honest beekeepers, processors, exporters, and consumers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of NBQP (The "Why")

1. **Assure quality** of Indian honey and bee products to domestic and international buyers.
2. **Boost exports** by aligning Indian honey with global residue and purity norms.
3. **Protect beekeepers** from unfair competition by adulterated/low-grade products.
4. **Establish traceability** from apiary → processing → packaging → consumer.
5. **Provide a unified Indian mark** to replace the patchwork of self-declarations.

## 2.2 Salient Features of NBQP

- **Voluntary in nature** — applicable to any FSSAI-licensed honey processor.
- **Product-specific standards** — the parameters differ for honey vs. beeswax vs. royal jelly.
- **Third-party audited** — assessment done by **NABL-accredited** laboratories and approved certifying agencies.
- **Traceability mandate** — every batch must be traceable to registered apiaries.
- **Renewable certification** — usually valid for **3 years**, with annual surveillance audits.
- **Distinct logo** — registered trademark; only authorised users may print it on labels.

## 2.3 Categories / Scope of Certification

| S.No. | Product Category | Standard Reference | Critical Parameters |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Extracted Honey (General) | IS 4941:1994 + FSSR 2011 | Moisture, HMF, Sucrose, Acidity |
| 2 | Monofloral Honey | NBQP Annexure I + IS 4941 | Pollen count + specific markers |
| 3 | Organic Honey | NBQP + NPOP (APEDA) | No antibiotic / pesticide residue |
| 4 | Beeswax | IS 5425:1989 | Melting point, saponification value |
| 5 | Royal Jelly | Codex / NBQP | 10-HDA content, freshness |
| 6 | Bee Pollen & Propolis | NBQP specifications | Moisture, microbial load |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Parameter Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists the **core honey parameters tested under NBQP**. Memorise the limits — these are favourite KTU question items.

| S.No. | Parameter | Symbol | NBQP/BIS Limit | Engineering / Analytical Significance |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content | $M$ | **≤ 20 %** | Indicates maturity & shelf-life; high $M$ → fermentation risk |
| 2 | Hydroxymethylfurfural | $HMF$ | **≤ 80 mg/kg** (tropical) | Marker of overheating / long storage |
| 3 | Sucrose | $S$ | **≤ 5 %** | Marker of adulteration with sugar syrup |
| 4 | Fructose + Glucose (Sum) | $F+G$ | **≥ 60 g/100 g** | Authenticity marker |
| 5 | Free Acidity | $A_f$ | **≤ 50 meq/kg** | Indicates fermentation / spoilage |
| 6 | Electrical Conductivity | $\sigma$ | **≤ 0.8 mS/cm** (for blossom honey) | Differentiates blossom vs. honeydew honey |
| 7 | Diastase Activity | $DA$ | **≥ 8 Schade units** | Indicator of freshness & minimal overheating |
| 8 | Antibiotic Residues | — | **Absent (LOD)** | Trade barrier for EU/US exports |
| 9 | Heavy Metals (Pb, Cd, As) | — | Within FSSR limits | Consumer safety |
| 10 | Pesticide Residues | — | Within FSSR limits | Consumer + export safety |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Food Technologists** apply NBQP parameters as design specifications during honey processing (dehumidification, pasteurisation temperature-time profiles).
- **Process Engineers** must size evaporators and heat exchangers to keep $HMF \leq 80~\text{mg/kg}$ while controlling microbial load.
- **Quality Managers** use the parameter sheet as the basis for HACCP critical limits.
- **Export Houses** require NBQP-aligned residue testing to satisfy EU Regulation 37/2010 and US FDA import alerts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** The diastase–HMF inverse relationship is the cornerstone of honey process design. The **Schade scale** is a kinetic activity index — controlling thermal exposure ($T, t$) preserves both freshness indicators simultaneously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HMF Formation as a Kinetic Process (Why Heat Control Matters)

Hydroxymethylfurfural is formed by the **acid-catalysed dehydration of fructose** during heating. It is the single most important *process indicator* in NBQP.

### Step 1 — Write the Governing Reaction

$$ \text{Fructose} \xrightarrow{\text{H}^+, \, \Delta T} \text{HMF} + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

### Step 2 — Assume First-Order Kinetics (Standard in Food Chemistry)

The rate of HMF accumulation follows the Arrhenius-type first-order law:

$$ \frac{d[HMF]}{dt} = k \cdot [\text{Fructose}]_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} $$

Where:
- $k$ = rate constant (1/min)
- $E_a$ = activation energy ($\approx 110$ kJ/mol for fructose dehydration)
- $R$ = universal gas constant $= 8.314~\text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### Step 3 — Integrate for an Isothermal Hold

Integrating from $t=0$ (with negligible initial HMF) to $t$:

$$ \begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{[HMF]} d[HMF] &= k \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot [\text{Fructose}]_0 \int_{0}^{t} dt \\
[HMF](t) &= k \cdot [\text{Fructose}]_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot t \\
\Rightarrow \quad [HMF](t) &= k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a/RT} \cdot t
\end{aligned} $$

This means **HMF grows linearly with time** at constant $T$, and **exponentially with temperature**.

### Step 4 — Solve the Typical Numerical (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Honey is heated at $T = 70\,^\circ\text{C}$ ($343\,\text{K}$) for $t = 30$ minutes. Given $E_a = 110{,}000~\text{J/mol}$, $R = 8.314~\text{J/(mol·K)}$, $k_0 = 9.5 \times 10^{14}~\text{min}^{-1}$, find the **HMF generated per kg honey** assuming initial HMF = 0.

**Solution:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
k &= k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \\
  &= 9.5 \times 10^{14} \cdot e^{-110000 / (8.314 \times 343)} \\
  &= 9.5 \times 10^{14} \cdot e^{-38.55} \\
  &= 9.5 \times 10^{14} \cdot 1.81 \times 10^{-17} \\
  &= 1.72 \times 10^{-2}~\text{min}^{-1}
\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}
[HMF] &= k \cdot t \quad (\text{for first-order, short isothermal hold}) \\
       &= (1.72 \times 10^{-2}) \times 30 \\
       &= 0.516~\text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned} $$

**Interpretation:** This is well below the NBQP limit of **80 mg/kg**. However, if the same honey is held at $90\,^\circ\text{C}$ for 60 minutes (a common mistake in poorly-designed pasteurisers), $k$ jumps by orders of magnitude, and $[HMF]$ may exceed the regulatory limit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Insight:** Always show the unit conversions (°C → K) and substitute numerical values step-by-step. KTU valuators award partial marks for **each correct substitution**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Design Decisions Linked to NBQP

| Engineering Decision | Relevant NBQP Parameter | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Pasteurisation temperature | HMF, Diastase | Higher $T$ kills yeast but damages enzymes |
| Storage humidity | Moisture content | High RH → $M$ increases → fermentation |
| Filter mesh size | Pollen count (monofloral certification) | Fine filter = clear honey but loses pollen |
| Cold-chain duration | HMF growth in storage | Each $10\,^\circ\text{C}$ rise doubles rate |
| Apiary registration | Residue testing (antibiotic/pesticide) | Trade-off vs. colony disease management |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NBQP Certification Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **end-to-end certification journey** of a honey processor applying for the NBQP mark. This is the kind of flowchart examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applicant Processor FSSAI Licensed] --> B[Online Application to NBB Portal]
    B --> C[Document Scrutiny by NBB]
    C --> D{Document OK?}
    D -- No --> E[Rectify and Resubmit within 30 Days]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Onsite Audit by Approved Certification Body]
    F --> G[Sample Drawing by Auditor]
    G --> H[Lab Testing at NABL Accredited Lab]
    H --> I{All NBQP Parameters within Limit?}
    I -- No --> J[Reject Application Inform Applicant]
    I -- Yes --> K[Issue NBQP Certificate Validity 3 Years]
    K --> L[Surveillance Audit Annual]
    L --> M{Continued Compliance?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Mark Continues]
    M -- No --> O[Suspension and Corrective Action]
    O --> P[Re-audit and Possible Reinstatement]
```

## 4.2 NBQP Parameter Hierarchy (Concept Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[NBQP Quality Mark] --> A[Physical Parameters]
    R1 --> B[Chemical Parameters]
    R1 --> C[Microbiological Parameters]
    R1 --> D[Residue Parameters]
    R1 --> E[Traceability and Documentation]

    A --> A1[Moisture]
    A --> A2[Electrical Conductivity]
    A --> A3[Optical Density]

    B --> B1[HMF]
    B --> B2[Diastase]
    B --> B3[Sucrose and Fructose plus Glucose]
    B --> B4[Free Acidity]

    C --> C1[Total Plate Count]
    C --> C2[Yeast and Mould]
    C --> C3[Coliforms]

    D --> D1[Antibiotic Residues]
    D --> D2[Heavy Metals]
    D --> D3[Pesticide Residues]

    E --> E1[Apiary Registration]
    E --> E2[Batch Traceability Records]
    E --> E3[HACCP Documentation]
```

## 4.3 Stakeholder Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Gov[Government Stakeholders]
        G1[NBB National Bee Board]
        G2[FSSAI]
        G3[BIS]
        G4[APEDA for Export]
    end

    subgraph Lab[Testing and Certification]
        L1[NABL Accredited Labs]
        L2[Approved Certification Bodies]
    end

    subgraph Ind[Industry Stakeholders]
        I1[Beekeepers]
        I2[Honey Processors]
        I3[Exporters]
        I4[Domestic Brands]
    end

    subgraph Cons[End Consumer]
        C1[Domestic Market]
        C2[International Market EU US Japan]
    end

    G1 --> L2
    G2 --> I2
    G3 --> L1
    G4 --> I3
    L1 --> L2
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> L2
    L2 --> I2
    I2 --> I3
    I2 --> I4
    I3 --> C2
    I4 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice the *triangular relationship* between **NBB → Certification Body → Processor**. The processor never directly interacts with the lab for certification purposes — independence is the cornerstone of NBQP's credibility.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEFTT742]`
**Define NBQP. Name the authority that administers it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NBQP stands for the **National Bee Board Quality Mark**. It is a voluntary, third-party quality certification mark for **honey and bee-hive products** manufactured or processed in India. The scheme is administered by the **National Bee Board (NBB)**, an autonomous body under the **Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, Government of India**. The mark assures that the product conforms to BIS Standards (e.g., IS 4941:1994 for extracted honey) and the FSSR 2011 specifications. *(3 marks — 1 for abbreviation, 1 for body, 1 for purpose.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEFTT742]`
**State any **four** critical honey parameters tested under NBQP with their permissible limits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark for each correct pair):**
1. Moisture content $\leq 20\%$
2. HMF $\leq 80~\text{mg/kg}$ (for tropical honey)
3. Sucrose $\leq 5\%$
4. Diastase activity $\geq 8$ Schade units
*(Any four from the cheat-sheet in Section 2.4 are acceptable.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks divided into sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Mark-split is shown against each sub-part.

---

### **Question A** — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **objectives, scope, and operational procedure** of the NBQP scheme as administered by the National Bee Board.

**Model Solution:**

**Objectives (2 marks):**
1. To assure consumers of honey quality and protect against adulteration.
2. To boost Indian honey exports by aligning with international residue and purity norms.
3. To provide a unified, government-endorsed domestic quality mark.
4. To establish traceability from apiary to retail pack.

**Scope (2 marks):**
The scheme covers **extracted honey, monofloral honey, organic honey, beeswax, royal jelly, bee pollen, and propolis**. It is **voluntary**, applicable to any **FSSAI-licensed honey processor**, exporter, or packager.

**Operational Procedure (3 marks — flowchart marks):**
The procedure consists of six sequential steps:
1. **Online application** by the processor on the NBB portal, attaching FSSAI license, HACCP records, and apiary sourcing details.
2. **Document scrutiny** by NBB officials.
3. **On-site audit** by an approved certification body (CB).
4. **Sample drawing and lab testing** at an NABL-accredited laboratory for all NBQP parameters.
5. **Certification** issued for **3 years** upon full compliance.
6. **Annual surveillance audits** to ensure continued compliance, with suspension and corrective action if non-conformities arise.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **critical chemical and residue parameters** tested under NBQP and explain the engineering relevance of controlling **HMF and moisture** during honey processing.

**Model Solution:**

**Critical Chemical Parameters (3 marks):**
- **Moisture (≤ 20 %):** Controls fermentation risk. Excess water enables osmophilic yeasts to multiply.
- **HMF (≤ 80 mg/kg):** A kinetic indicator of overheating or prolonged storage.
- **Sucrose (≤ 5 %):** Detects adulteration with sugar syrups.
- **Fructose + Glucose sum (≥ 60 g/100 g):** Authenticity marker.
- **Free acidity (≤ 50 meq/kg):** Indicates fermentation / spoilage.
- **Diastase activity (≥ 8 Schade units):** Indicator of freshness and minimal thermal damage.

**Residue Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Antibiotic residues** (chloramphenicol, nitrofurans) — *must be absent* at limit of detection, per EU/US import norms.
- **Heavy metals** (Pb, Cd, As) — within FSSR limits.
- **Pesticide residues** — within FSSR limits.

**Engineering Relevance of HMF and Moisture Control (2 marks):**
- HMF follows **first-order Arrhenius kinetics** with respect to temperature, doubling approximately every $10\,^\circ\text{C}$ rise. Process engineers must therefore design **HTST pasteurisers** ($T \approx 65\,^\circ\text{C}$, $t \approx 30$ min) rather than conventional batch heating.
- Moisture is reduced through **vacuum evaporation** under controlled $T$ and vacuum ($P$) to keep $T < 50\,^\circ\text{C}$, simultaneously protecting diastase and reducing $M$.

---

### **Question B** — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **NBQP scheme with the BIS Hallmarking scheme** for gold jewellery. Highlight at least **five points of similarity and contrast** in their operational design.

**Model Solution:**

The BIS Hallmark (gold) and the NBQP (honey) are both **government-endorsed third-party quality certification marks** in India. Their comparison:

| Dimension | BIS Hallmark (Gold) | NBQP (Honey) |
|---|---|---|
| Administering Body | **Bureau of Indian Standards** | **National Bee Board** |
| Voluntary or Mandatory? | **Mandatory** (since 2021) | **Voluntary** |
| Product Scope | Gold jewellery of specific carats | Honey & bee-hive products |
| Lab Network | BIS-recognised assaying centres | NABL-accredited labs |
| Audit Cadence | Periodic surveillance | Annual surveillance, 3-year validity |
| Traceability | Jeweller ID + hallmark number | Batch number + apiary source |
| Consumer Trust Signal | Caratage purity (22K, 18K) | Compliance with 10+ parameters |
| Common Strength | Both protect consumer from fraud | Both protect honest trade |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Both schemes use the same **three pillars** — *Government endorsement + Independent testing + Traceability* — to deliver trust. The difference is largely in **regulatory weight** (mandatory vs. voluntary).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A honey processor plans to install a **continuous HTST pasteuriser** for honey. The product enters at $25\,^\circ\text{C}$, is heated to $65\,^\circ\text{C}$, held for 30 seconds, and cooled to $30\,^\circ\text{C}$. Using the first-order HMF kinetics equation given in Section 3.1, estimate the **HMF generated per kg**, and **comment on whether the process complies with NBQP** ($k_0 = 9.5 \times 10^{14}~\text{min}^{-1}$, $E_a = 110{,}000~\text{J/mol}$).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the critical time-temperature phase:**
The hot-hold phase at $65\,^\circ\text{C}$ ($338~\text{K}$) for $30$ s ($0.5$ min) is the dominant HMF-generating stage. Heating and cooling ramps contribute negligibly and are ignored.

**Step 2 — Compute $k$ at $T = 338~\text{K}$:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
k &= k_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT} \\
  &= 9.5 \times 10^{14} \cdot e^{-110000 / (8.314 \times 338)} \\
  &= 9.5 \times 10^{14} \cdot e^{-39.13} \\
  &= 9.5 \times 10^{14} \cdot 1.12 \times 10^{-17} \\
  &= 1.07 \times 10^{-2}~\text{min}^{-1}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 3 — Compute HMF generated:**

$$ \begin{aligned}
[HMF] &= k \cdot t \\
       &= (1.07 \times 10^{-2}) \times 0.5 \\
       &= 5.35 \times 10^{-3}~\text{mg/kg}
\end{aligned} $$

**Step 4 — Compare with NBQP limit:**
- Generated HMF = $0.00535~\text{mg/kg}$
- NBQP limit = $80~\text{mg/kg}$
- Margin of safety = $80 / 0.00535 \approx 14{,}953\times$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The HTST process is **vastly compliant** with NBQP. The HTST design is, in fact, the **engineering best practice** because it minimises HMF generation while still achieving yeast inactivation (yeast $D_{65\,^\circ\text{C}} \approx 5$–$10$ s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting to **convert °C to Kelvin** (loses 2 marks in any numerical).
> 2. Confusing **NBQP** with **NPOP** (organic) or **NABL** (lab accreditation) — they are entirely different schemes.
> 3. Writing "NBQP is mandatory" — it is **VOLUNTARY**; deduct 1 mark.
> 4. Skipping the **traceability** aspect when describing objectives — examiners specifically test this.
> 5. In numericals, presenting $k$ without **units** loses 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Full Form:** National Bee Board Quality Mark.
- **Administered by:** National Bee Board (NBB), Ministry of Agriculture.
- **Type:** **Voluntary**, third-party assured.
- **First launch:** **2018** (revamped scheme).
- **Product scope:** Honey, beeswax, royal jelly, pollen, propolis.
- **Key standard:** IS 4941:1994 (extracted honey).
- **Moisture limit:** ≤ 20 %.
- **HMF limit (tropical):** ≤ 80 mg/kg.
- **Sucrose limit:** ≤ 5 %.
- **Diastase:** ≥ 8 Schade units.
- **Conductivity:** ≤ 0.8 mS/cm (blossom honey).
- **Lab:** NABL-accredited only.
- **Certificate validity:** 3 years, annual surveillance.
- **Mandatory tests:** Antibiotic residues (chloramphenicol, nitrofurans) — must be absent.
- **Engineering link:** HMF obeys first-order Arrhenius kinetics with $E_a \approx 110~\text{kJ/mol}$.
- **Best practice:** HTST pasteurisation $65\,^\circ\text{C}$ / 30 s.
- **Don't confuse with:** NPOP (organic), NABL (lab), BIS Hallmark (gold), Agmark (agricultural commodities).
- **Why it matters:** Differentiates pure honey from adulterated product in domestic + export markets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
